caveats: a: OEWS source data are for NAICS 238900, not six-digit 238910, exclude self-employed workers, and are 2023 vintage.
a: Task exposure within each occupation is judgmental; no source directly reports a wage-weighted AI-exposable share for this industry.

a: The injected labor ratio l is LOW quality, uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts at ancestor 23891, and is harmonized to the IPS basis by a cross-code median divisor; that uncertainty affects the eventual labor-opportunity construct even though it does not change a itself.
rho: RICS is global and only 10% of its 2,200-plus responses came from the Americas.
rho: AGC reports firm use or investment plans, not the share of exposed tasks successfully implemented or benefits sustained.
rho: Implementation is likely heterogeneous: back-office tools should move faster than autonomous equipment, safety, or live field-control workflows.
e: The industry includes distinct demolition, excavation, septic, drilling, and equipment-with-operator niches, though all share a project-service and accountable-operator model.
e: Eligibility cannot be observed from NAICS classification alone, and owner dependence is especially hard to distinguish from a genuinely transferable operating platform.
e: The injected n of 4,678 is itself an ESTIMATE margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using an Engineering/Construction EBITDA margin; e is layered on that uncertain band count.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures owner intentions rather than completed transfers or continued operating viability after transfer.
s5: The FMI/CFMA figures are reported through an accounting-firm article and concern contractors broadly, not site-preparation firms or only the LMM band.
s5: Family gifts, minority recapitalizations, closures, deaths without transferable operations, and internal reorganizations are not qualifying control transfers and are excluded conceptually.
q: The contract evidence comes from a larger public civil contractor and may not match the residential, septic, or small commercial mix of LMM site-preparation firms.
q: No source directly measures AI-derived benefit pass-through, renewal repricing, or competitor response in this industry.
q: Project mix, local bidder density, bonding capacity, utilization, differing site conditions, and change-order discipline can move retention widely.
d5: The base uses total construction real output because BLS does not publish six-digit 238910 output projections.
d5: Employment is only a directional demand check and can diverge from service quantity because of productivity, overtime, labor shortages, or occupation substitution.
d5: Site preparation is cyclical and locally exposed to housing, commercial construction, public budgets, weather, interest rates, permitting, and infrastructure-policy renewal.
o: The estimate distinguishes operator-required demand from labor intensity: fewer labor hours per project do not by themselves eliminate the accountable contractor.
o: Autonomous equipment could advance faster than expected in fenced, repetitive, greenfield environments, but fragmented sites and legacy fleets should slow complete disintermediation.
o: OSHA requires competent-person functions for excavation safety, but that person need not be the business owner and regulations could evolve.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based services sold to general contractors, developers, property owners, utilities, and public agencies, commonly through competitively bid lump-sum or fixed-unit-price contracts, with some negotiated and cost-plus work. Recurrence is repeat outsourcing across successive projects rather than subscription revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Land subdivision and real-estate development, construction-equipment rental without an operator, crane rental with an operator, utility-line construction, underwater trenching, mining overburden removal, hazardous-material remediation, captive internal sitework units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors performing outsourced land clearing, grading, excavation, earthmoving, trenching, demolition, dewatering, septic-system installation, foundation digging or drilling, and construction-equipment rental with an operator for repeat customers or successive projects.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 2023 occupation mix for the broader NAICS 238900 parent, not AI-exposed tasks in 238910. I treat most construction-laborer and equipment-operator wages as minimally exposed, apply meaningful partial exposure to management, estimating, project management, office, bookkeeping, dispatch, and documentation work, and allow a small exposure for machine guidance and field reporting. This likely overstates near-term substitution of field tasks but may understate emerging autonomy and computer-vision assistance; the parent group also contains non-site-preparation trades.
caveats: OEWS source data are for NAICS 238900, not six-digit 238910, exclude self-employed workers, and are 2023 vintage.
Task exposure within each occupation is judgmental; no source directly reports a wage-weighted AI-exposable share for this industry.

The injected labor ratio l is LOW quality, uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts at ancestor 23891, and is harmonized to the IPS basis by a cross-code median divisor; that uncertainty affects the eventual labor-opportunity construct even though it does not change a itself.
a.change_evidence: A current OEWS special tabulation for NAICS 238910 combined with O*NET task-level hours and payroll by role, plus time-and-motion studies at 20-30 LMM excavation, demolition, and septic contractors separating estimating, dispatch, compliance, project controls, and field hours, could materially move this range.
a.rationale: The parent-industry mix is dominated by embodied field work: construction and extraction occupations are 62.30% of employment, including construction laborers at 22.87% and equipment operators at 16.81%. The more digitally addressable pools include management at 5.85%, business and financial operations at 3.61% (including cost estimators and project-management specialists), and office support at 8.50%. Wage-weighting those groups and assigning partial rather than whole-role exposure yields a bounded 16% base. RICS identifies scheduling, progress monitoring, cost estimating, and documentation-adjacent functions as practical AI use cases, while BLS describes core equipment work as physical, site-specific machine control.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for total construction, employment for specialty trade contractors, and employment for equipment operators rather than constant-quality quantity demand for NAICS 238910. I convert total-construction real output growth from 2024 to 2034 into a five-year ratio using the square root of the ten-year ratio, yielding about 1.04, and use specialty-trade and equipment-operator projections as directional checks. Broad construction may understate infrastructure-led sitework but can overstate 238910 if building cycles weaken or productivity rises.
caveats: The base uses total construction real output because BLS does not publish six-digit 238910 output projections.
Employment is only a directional demand check and can diverge from service quantity because of productivity, overtime, labor shortages, or occupation substitution.
Site preparation is cyclical and locally exposed to housing, commercial construction, public budgets, weather, interest rates, permitting, and infrastructure-policy renewal.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of constant-dollar excavation, grading, demolition, septic, and sitework put-in-place by end market and state, ideally from Dodge Construction Network or ConstructConnect and reconciled to Census Value of Construction Put in Place, would materially change d5; contractor backlog and bid-volume panels would sharpen timing.
d5.rationale: BLS projects construction output in chained 2017 dollars from $1,720.9 billion in 2024 to $1,874.7 billion in 2034; the corresponding five-year geometric ratio is about 1.04. It also projects specialty-trade employment up 4.3% and construction-equipment-operator employment up 4% over the decade, and states that infrastructure repair and population-driven roads and sewer lines should generate operator jobs. IIJA's $550 billion of new federal infrastructure investment through fiscal 2026 supports the near-term pipeline, but private construction cyclicality and post-IIJA funding uncertainty justify a downside below current demand.

caveats: The industry includes distinct demolition, excavation, septic, drilling, and equipment-with-operator niches, though all share a project-service and accountable-operator model.
Eligibility cannot be observed from NAICS classification alone, and owner dependence is especially hard to distinguish from a genuinely transferable operating platform.
The injected n of 4,678 is itself an ESTIMATE margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using an Engineering/Construction EBITDA margin; e is layered on that uncertain band count.
e.change_evidence: A stratified sample of NAICS 238910 tax returns or confidential Census microdata linked to customer concentration, outside-customer revenue, employee count, bonding, fleet ownership, and owner hours, supplemented by 50 broker teasers or quality-of-earnings reports, could directly estimate the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is overwhelmingly external, operator-provided project service: land clearing, drilling, demolition, trenching, earthmoving, and equipment rental with operators. Most firms already in the injected EBITDA band therefore fit the repeat-outsourced-service lens. The discount removes captive operations, firms whose economics depend almost entirely on an owner-operator, businesses dominated by excluded adjacent activities, and operations lacking transferable customer, workforce, equipment, bonding, or safety systems.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23891 ($29,862,904,244) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23891 ($144,455,874,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23891. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $331,445 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238910); class <5: 23,625 firms, avg revenue/firm $467,933 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $37,247 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,465 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,722,382 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $137,102 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,357 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,860,528 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $307,298 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 4,094 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,126,455 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,044,866 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 584 firms, avg revenue/firm $60,891,793 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,846,987 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 117 firms, avg revenue/firm $137,990,769 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,984,065 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 4,678

caveats: The estimate distinguishes operator-required demand from labor intensity: fewer labor hours per project do not by themselves eliminate the accountable contractor.
Autonomous equipment could advance faster than expected in fenced, repetitive, greenfield environments, but fragmented sites and legacy fleets should slow complete disintermediation.
OSHA requires competent-person functions for excavation safety, but that person need not be the business owner and regulations could evolve.
o.change_evidence: Commercial deployment data for autonomous excavation and grading systems—hours run without onboard operators, remote-supervisor ratios, incident and intervention rates, project types, and contractor-of-record arrangements—plus owner and general-contractor procurement interviews could materially change o.
o.rationale: The service basket physically changes land and structures using heavy equipment and crews under changing soil, utility, weather, access, and safety conditions. Software can improve bids, machine guidance, monitoring, and coordination, and partial autonomy can reduce labor per unit, but it does not eliminate the contractor responsible for mobilization, equipment, field execution, safety, quality, and remediation. OSHA's excavation rules require trained, authorized competent-person judgment and inspection, reinforcing persistent operator accountability.

caveats: The contract evidence comes from a larger public civil contractor and may not match the residential, septic, or small commercial mix of LMM site-preparation firms.
No source directly measures AI-derived benefit pass-through, renewal repricing, or competitor response in this industry.
Project mix, local bidder density, bonding capacity, utilization, differing site conditions, and change-order discipline can move retention widely.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level bid histories and gross-margin bridges from 20-30 LMM site-preparation contractors, separated into lump-sum, unit-price, time-and-materials, and cost-plus work, plus renewal or rebid cohorts before and after automation, would identify actual pass-through and materially change q.
q.rationale: Site-preparation work is often competitively bid under lump-sum or fixed-unit-price terms. An incumbent can initially retain savings on a fixed-price job when actual costs fall below the bid, but frequent rebidding, transparent unit prices, general-contractor procurement, and imitation push part of repeatable savings to customers through lower bids. Cost-plus work passes savings through more directly. The base therefore retains less than half of a discounted five-year gross benefit, with higher retention possible for differentiated local operators facing constrained capacity or opaque site risk.

rho.bridge: RICS surveys global construction professionals and AGC surveys construction firms, not US LMM site-preparation contractors or the fraction of exposed wage opportunity implemented. I translate current adoption, pilot activity, use cases, and barriers into a five-year implementation share, discounting for smaller-firm data fragmentation, integration, workforce skills, safety review, and the difficulty of changing field workflows. The AGC sample may bias upward toward larger, more organized firms, while falling software costs could make the estimate conservative.
caveats: RICS is global and only 10% of its 2,200-plus responses came from the Americas.
AGC reports firm use or investment plans, not the share of exposed tasks successfully implemented or benefits sustained.
Implementation is likely heterogeneous: back-office tools should move faster than autonomous equipment, safety, or live field-control workflows.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal panel of LMM NAICS 238910 firms reporting baseline and post-deployment labor hours, adoption by workflow, error and rework rates, human-review requirements, and sustained use after 12-24 months would materially change rho; vendor telemetry segmented by contractor revenue and trade would be a practical substitute.
rho.rationale: RICS found 45% of respondents with no AI implementation, 34% in early pilots, just under 12% using AI regularly in specific processes, and less than 1% fully embedded enterprise-wide; it also found 78% at no or pilot-stage adoption and 74% with limited or no preparation. In contrast, AGC's 2026 outlook reports 61% using AI or planning increased investment, with 45% using it for office and administrative work and 23% for estimating. Over five years, packaged estimating, document, dispatch, accounting, and project-control tools should operationalize about half of the exposed opportunity, but full realization is constrained by messy job data, integration, review obligations, and change capacity.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among all US employer-business owners, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM site-preparation firms. I start from the observed 22% employer-owner plan rate, remove planned gifts or transfers that may not change control and plans that fail, and then partly offset that haircut for established LMM contractors and construction succession pressure. This approach likely overstates realized transfers because intentions do not always close, but may understate construction-specific retirement pressure.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures owner intentions rather than completed transfers or continued operating viability after transfer.
The FMI/CFMA figures are reported through an accounting-firm article and concern contractors broadly, not site-preparation firms or only the LMM band.
Family gifts, minority recapitalizations, closures, deaths without transferable operations, and internal reorganizations are not qualifying control transfers and are excluded conceptually.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-transaction data from BizBuySell, GF Data, Axial, PitchBook, and construction-focused brokers coded to NAICS 238910, matched to the opening stock of eligible firms and including internal sales, ESOPs, family transfers, recapitalizations, and failed listings over 2021-2026, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup's fall-2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9% of nonemployers. Construction evidence points to a material but poorly prepared succession pipeline: a report citing the 2024 FMI/CFMA survey says 58% of responding contractors lacked an ownership-transition plan and half of those expecting retirement within three to five years had no plan. A 17% base recognizes that planned transfers include gifts and failed processes, while LMM scale, equipment, employees, and repeat relationships generally improve transferability relative to owner-only businesses.

principal_driver: AI-assisted estimating, project controls, dispatch, and administration can expand scarce management capacity without displacing the accountable field operator.
principal_weakness: The addressable wage layer is modest and savings are vulnerable to competitive rebidding in a cyclical, owner- and execution-dependent industry.
